serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Baingan Bharta is approximately 200 g (roughly one medium bowl), which is a typical portion when served alongside rotis or parathas as part of a North Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Baingan Bharta are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using the cooked eggplant dish profile with added oil and spices. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with ICMR-NIN published values for cooked brinjal preparations. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked eggplant with oil (FDC ID 169228) to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — eggplant / aubergine reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Baingan Bharta exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated from the closest analogous food: cooked eggplant / aubergine, which is consistently reported as very low GI (approximately 10–20) in peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021). Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (15 × 16.66) / 100 ≈ 2.5. This is classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt during cooking
hypertension.guidance: Baingan Bharta contains approximately 150 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one medium bowl (200 g) to keep sodium intake within recommended daily limits, while benefiting from the potassium content (210 mg/100 g) which supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and 2.67 g dietary fibre per 100 g, Baingan Bharta produces a minimal postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish as a regular part of their meal plan, pairing it with low-GI breads such as bajra rotla rather than refined wheat rotis to further moderate glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of antioxidant compounds from eggplant and spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baingan Bharta is low in saturated fat (0.83 g/100 g) and contains zero cholesterol; the eggplant provides anthocyanins and chlorogenic acid with antioxidant properties, and turmeric contributes curcumin with anti-inflammatory effects, making this dish a heart-friendly choice when prepared with moderate amounts of oil (preferably mustard or olive oil) and limited added salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable content (eggplant, tomato) may trigger inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While turmeric in Baingan Bharta has anti-inflammatory properties, eggplant and tomatoes are nightshade vegetables that some individuals with rheumatoid arthritis report as symptom triggers; patients should monitor their individual response and consider an elimination trial if joint symptoms worsen after consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baingan Bharta is a traditional North Indian vegetarian dish, most closely associated with Punjabi cuisine, made from whole eggplants (baingan) that are fire-roasted directly over an open flame or under a grill until the skin is completely charred and the interior flesh becomes soft and smoky. The charred skin is peeled away and the flesh is mashed, then sautéed in oil with finely chopped onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and a blend of ground spices including turmeric, red chili powder, coriander powder, and garam masala, finished with fresh coriander leaves. The defining characteristic of the dish is its deep, smoky aroma imparted by the direct-flame roasting technique, which distinguishes it from other eggplant preparations. Nutritionally, Baingan Bharta provides approximately 93 kcal per 100 g, with 2 g protein, 6 g fat, and 8.33 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-calorie, low-carbohydrate dish. It is rich in dietary fibre (2.67 g/100 g), potassium (210 mg/100 g), and contains antioxidants including anthocyanins from eggplant and curcumin from turmeric. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. It is traditionally served with bajra rotla, wheat roti, or paratha, and is a staple of home-cooked North Indian meals at lunch and dinner. The dish is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. It is sometimes compared to Middle Eastern Baba Ganoush but differs significantly in its spiced, sautéed tempering and the use of Indian aromatics.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods', which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes. No exact Baingan Bharta composite dish entry exists in ICMR-NIN; values are derived from the cooked brinjal preparation profile with added oil and spices, consistent with standard recipe composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked eggplant as the reference food. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Baingan Bharta is not available in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific fire-roasted and sautéed preparation is not reported in the primary source. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a plant-based dish prepared with vegetable or mustard oil; consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA data for cooked eggplant preparations. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based vegan dish containing no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero for this plant-based preparation; eggplant and the spices used contain negligible vitamin D. 7. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked eggplant/aubergine from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021); no direct GI measurement for Baingan Bharta exists in the database. 8. Portions: the input contained duplicate '1 serving (200g)' entries; deduplicated to a single 200 g serving as the default. The 300 g bowl portion from the input is retained as an alternate serving size. 9. Ontology: FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate anchors; no exact composite dish codes exist for Baingan Bharta in either system as of 2024.
